skill.name: biopython-sequence-io
skill.description: Use Biopython to read/write/convert biological sequence files (FASTA/GenBank/FASTQ, etc.) and perform basic sequence operations; use when you need reliable sequence I/O, lightweight sequence manipulation, or scalable processing of large sequence datasets.
skill.summary: Read, write, convert, and filter biological sequences using Biopython SeqIO with support for FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, and other formats.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 100 static findings are false positives. The scanner confused Python code blocks in markdown with Ruby backtick execution, misidentified sequence record ID access as system reconnaissance, and flagged legitimate Biopython references as weak cryptographic algorithms. This is a legitimate bioinformatics skill that uses Biopython for sequence file I/O. The heuristic critical finding for dangerous combination is also a false positive: there is no actual code execution beyond documented Python script usage, no credential access, and only a source URL citation for network. The prior audit confirms all security veto gates passed.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm detected by scanner
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at 8 locations. All are false positives. The scanner likely confused Biopython function and variable names with cryptographic keyword patterns. Line references point to Seq object creation (references/sequence_io.md:5), record.description assignment (references/sequence_io.md:104,116,280), and SKILL.md description text (SKILL.md:3,160,173) and audit JSON score notes (biopython-sequence-io_audit_result_v2.json:72). None contain actual cryptographic operations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines contain standard Biopython code and markdown documentation text with no cryptographic operations. Scanner produced false match on non-crypto keywords.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/shell backtick execution detected by scanner
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 77 locations for 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All are false positives. The files contain Python code examples in markdown code blocks (```python) and plain text, not Ruby backticks. The skill is a Python/Biopython skill. Examples include SeqIO.parse() calls, SeqRecord manipulation, and Python function definitions in references/sequence_io.md and SKILL.md.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged content is Python code or markdown text. The scanner pattern-matched code block formatting characters as Ruby backtick syntax.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: SQLite database file reference
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md:44 for 'SQLite database file'. This is a legitimate configuration example for Biopython's SeqIO.index_db() which uses SQLite as an index backend for fast random access to very large sequence files. This is standard bioinformatics practice, not a security concern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SQLite reference is in a JSON config example for Biopython's built-in database indexing feature. No actual database connection string or credentials are present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System reconnaissance detected by scanner
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 8 locations for 'System reconnaissance'. All are false positives involving sequence record ID access in Biopython code examples. References like record.id (references/sequence_io.md:102,115) and list comprehensions extracting IDs (references/sequence_io.md:204) are standard biological sequence operations, not system reconnaissance. Audit result log messages (biopython-sequence-io_audit_result_v2.json:23,120) and SKILL.md documentation lines are also innocuous.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines reference biological sequence record identifiers or documentation comments. None resemble system reconnaissance or host discovery.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL citation
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged SKILL.md:7 for 'Hardcoded URL'. This is a legitimate source citation linking to the skill's GitHub repository. The URL https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills is the skill's source repository, not a malicious endpoint. No credentials or sensitive data are transmitted.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a reference to the authoritative source repository for this skill. Standard attribution practice with no security risk.

content.user_title: Read, write, and convert biological sequences with Biopython
content.value_statement: Researchers often need to process biological sequence data across different file formats, but writing custom parsers is error-prone and time-consuming. This skill uses Biopython SeqIO to reliably read, write, and convert FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, and other sequence formats while preserving annotations and identifiers.
actual_capabilities: Read and parse biological sequence files in FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, EMBL, and SwissProt formats using Bio.SeqIO
Write sequences to output files in any supported format with proper record formatting and annotation preservation
Convert between sequence formats such as FASTA to GenBank or FASTQ to FASTA while keeping identifiers intact
Perform basic sequence operations including reverse complement, transcription, and translation using Bio.Seq
Filter sequences by length, GC content, and ambiguous base thresholds during streaming processing
Process large sequence files efficiently using iterator-based parsing with optional indexed access via SeqIO.index()
limitations: Cannot read proprietary sequence formats not supported by the Biopython SeqIO library
Complex annotation structures in GenBank may not fully transfer to simpler target formats like FASTA during conversion
Memory usage can be high when using SeqIO.to_dict() for random access on large files instead of iterator-based approaches
Does not perform sequence alignment, phylogenetic analysis, genome assembly, or structural biology computations

use_cases.title: Convert sequence files between formats
use_cases.description: Convert a batch of GenBank files to FASTA format so they can be used as input for alignment or assembly tools that require FASTA input. All record identifiers are preserved during conversion.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics researchers preparing data for downstream analysis pipelines

use_cases.title: Filter sequencing reads by quality
use_cases.description: Filter FASTQ sequencing reads by minimum PHRED quality score threshold and write only high-quality reads to a new file. Generate a summary with counts of total, filtered, and kept reads.
use_cases.target_user: Genomics technicians processing raw sequencing data from laboratory instruments

use_cases.title: Process sequence statistics from large collections
use_cases.description: Extract sequence identifiers, compute GC content and length statistics, and filter sequences by criteria from large sequence collections for reporting or creating analysis subsets.
use_cases.target_user: Lab scientists and researchers who need to audit, organize, and subsample sequence datasets

prompt_templates.title: Basic format conversion with filtering
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the FASTA file at data/sequences.fasta to GenBank format and save the result as data/sequences.gb. Filter out any sequences shorter than 100 base pairs during the conversion.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user converting a single file between common sequence formats

prompt_templates.title: Quality-aware FASTQ processing with summary report
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the FASTQ file at data/reads.fastq and filter out reads where the minimum PHRED quality score is below 20. Write the filtered reads to data/filtered.fastq and generate a summary report with total reads, filtered reads, and mean read length.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user filtering sequencing reads by quality score thresholds

prompt_templates.title: Indexed access for large genome files
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an index for the large FASTA file at data/genome.fasta using SeqIO.index() and use it to retrieve sequences for IDs geneA, geneB, and geneC. Write the retrieved sequences to a new FASTA output file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user working with large genome-scale sequence files requiring random access

prompt_templates.title: Batch multi-format processing pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Process all files in the data/ directory. Detect their format by file extension, parse each file, compute GC content and length statistics per record, write a consolidated CSV summary, and convert all records to FASTA in a single output file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert user building an automated multi-file sequence processing pipeline

output_examples.input: Convert a FASTA file containing 3 DNA sequences to GenBank format
output_examples.output: Created GenBank file from 3 FASTA sequences. Each record preserved its original ID and nucleotide sequence. Output file data/sequences.gb contains 3 records with GC fraction annotations attached.

output_examples.input: Filter FASTQ reads with minimum quality score of 20 for downstream processing
output_examples.output: Processed 50000 reads from data/reads.fastq. Filtered out 3200 reads below the quality threshold. Wrote 46800 high-quality reads to data/filtered.fastq. Mean read length after filtering was 151 base pairs.

best_practices: Use iterator-based parsing with SeqIO.parse() for large files instead of loading everything into memory to avoid crashes
Use SeqIO.index() for repeated random access to large sequence files instead of SeqIO.to_dict() which loads all records
Always validate input sequences before writing output to avoid corrupting the target file with malformed records
anti_patterns: Calling SeqIO.to_dict() on multi-gigabyte sequence files which exhausts available memory and causes the process to crash
Modifying record.seq directly without creating a new SeqRecord or preserving annotations, leading to inconsistent metadata
Specifying format strings with mixed case like 'FASTA' or 'GenBank' instead of lowercase 'fasta' or 'genbank' which Biopython does not recognize

faq.question: What sequence file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports all formats handled by Biopython SeqIO including FASTA, FASTQ, GenBank, EMBL, and SwissProt. Always use lowercase format strings when specifying a format.

faq.question: Can I convert a GenBank file to FASTA format?
faq.answer: Yes. Use SeqIO.convert with input format 'genbank' and output format 'fasta'. Note that GenBank annotations like features and references will be lost because FASTA only stores identifiers and sequences.

faq.question: How do I process very large sequence files without running out of memory?
faq.answer: Use SeqIO.parse() which returns an iterator instead of loading the entire file. For random access, use SeqIO.index() for lazy loading or SeqIO.index_db() with SQLite for files with millions of records.

faq.question: Does this work with compressed or gzipped sequence files?
faq.answer: Yes. Biopython SeqIO automatically handles gzip-compressed files. You can use .gz files with the same format string and SeqIO decompresses them transparently during parsing.

faq.question: Can I compute GC content for sequences with this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a gc_fraction function in the example script and also recommends using Bio.SeqUtils.gc_fraction for computing GC content per record during processing.

faq.question: What are the software dependencies required to use this skill?
faq.answer: Requires Python 3 with Biopython version 1.80 or higher and NumPy version 1.21 or higher. The skill runs on any operating system that supports these packages.
